request.question: Which board game won more awards in 1983, Scotland Yard or Shengguan Tu?

title: Scotland Yard vs. Dr. Mabuse
Stryker of the Yard
Black Museum
Psychopath (1968 film)
Commissioners of Scotland Yard
Scotland Yard (band)
Shengguan Tu
Fantômas contre Scotland Yard
Scotland Yard (board game)
221B Baker Street (board game)
sentences: Scotland Yard vs. Dr Mabuse (German: Scotland Yard jagt Dr. Mabuse , also known as "Scotland Yard Hunts Dr. Mabuse") is a 1963 German crime film directed by Paul May and starring Peter van Eyck.

Stryker of the Yard (A.K.A. Stryker of Scotland Yard and Scotland Yard Cases ) is a 1953 British crime film directed by Arthur Crabtree and starring Clifford Evans, Susan Stephen, Jack Watling and Eliot Makeham.
The Black Museum, or The Crime Museum of Scotland Yard, is a collection of criminal memorabilia kept at New Scotland Yard, headquarters of the Metropolitan Police Service in London, England.
The museum came into existence sometime in 1874, although unofficially.
It was housed at Scotland Yard, and grew from the collection of prisoners' property gathered under the authority of the Prisoners Property Act of 1869.
The act was intended to help the police in their study of crime and criminals.
By 1875, it had become an official museum, although not open to the public, with a police inspector and a police constable assigned to official duty there.
Psychopath (Italian: "Sigpress contro Scotland Yard" , German: "Mister Zehn Prozent - Miezen und Moneten" ) is a 1968 Italian-German film directed by .
The film is about Sigpress (Francisco Martinez Celeiro), a thief who steals jewels which he then returns to the owners in order to collect money as a reward.
He is being tracked by Scotland Yard when he learns that the gem called the Eye of Allah is being escorted from London to Paris.
On attempting to steal the jewel, he finds it's a fake which leads to his own investigation.

The Commissioners of Scotland Yard was the informal name for the Commissioners for the Streets and Wayes, a body of improvement commissioners established in 1662 to manage and regulate various areas relating to streets and traffic in the cities of London and Westminster and the borough of Southwark.
They were appointed under a 1662 Act of the Parliament of England (14 Car.
2 c. 2) which expired in 1679.
The Commissioners' office was attached to that of the Surveyor of the King's Works in Scotland Yard.
Scotland Yard is a pop rock group from Los Angeles, California: Scotland Yard began when Chris Hill began demoing songs in 1989 for a female fronted new wave pop group.
In 1991 Kim Cahill responded to an out of date ad for a singer in a local industry trade magazine.
The edgy pop rock sound from the duo would catch an ear in the Los Angeles and Orange county music scene.

Shengguan Tu (), translated variously as Promoting Officials and Table of Bureaucratic Promotion, is an ancient Chinese board game that originated in the Tang dynasty, with the earliest historical record of a variant of it dating back to 836.

Compared to "Monopoly" and snakes and ladders by contemporary commentators, the game has players take on the roles of mandarins; the objective of the game is to attain the highest possible bureaucratic position, whilst accumulating more prestige and monetary funds than other players.
Historically a gambling game doubling as an educational tool for acquainting Chinese males with the bureaucratic hierarchy, it still enjoys relative popularity nowadays.

Fantômas contre Scotland Yard (] , "Fantomas Against Scotland Yard") is the final installment of a trilogy of films starring Jean Marais as the arch villain with the same name opposite Louis de Funès as the earnest but outclassed commissaire Juve and the journalist Fandor, also played by Marais.
The trilogy was France's humorous answer, starting in 1964, to the James Bond phenomenon that swept the world at around the same time.
The "Fantômas" films became extremely successful in Europe and USSR, and found success even in the United States and Japan where fan websites exist to this day.
Scotland Yard is a board game in which a team of players, as police, cooperate to track down a player controlling a criminal around a board representing the streets of London.
It is named after Scotland Yard, the headquarters of London's Metropolitan Police Service.
"Scotland Yard" is an asymmetric board game, with the detective players cooperatively solving a variant of the pursuit-evasion problem.
The game is published by Ravensburger in most of Europe and Canada and by Milton Bradley in the United States.
It received the "Spiel des Jahres" (Game of the Year) award in 1983.

221B Baker Street: The Master Detective Game is a board game featuring Arthur Conan Doyle's fictional detective Sherlock Holmes and developed by Jay Moriarty (dba Antler Productions) in 1975 and sold by the John N. Hansen Co. in the US since 1977.
The players have to solve cases using the clues provided by visiting locations on the board such as 221B Baker Street, Scotland Yard, Apothecary and Pawn Broker.
The original game has twenty cases, but there are eight expansion sets of 20 cases each for a total of 180 cases available for play.
A new Deluxe Edition of 221 B Baker Street, published by Hansen, has been released in the U.S. in 2016-17.
This Deluxe Edition contains the original 180 Cases plus 20 all-new never-before-solved Cases for a Complete Set of 200 Cases.
The Deluxe Edition also includes all new artwork and collectible Sherlockian metal tokens.
The original game has been licensed for sale in the UK (Gibsons Games), Canada, Australia, New Zealand and Brazil.
Editions of the game published by Gibson have reproduced 40 of the 180 cases of the Hansen edition, but also include 80 new cases.
The Brazil version is titled Scotland Yard and translated in Portuguese.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Which board game won more awards in 1983, Scotland Yard or Shengguan Tu?
hop1_docs: Scotland Yard (board game) | Scotland Yard is a board game in which a team of players, as police, cooperate to track down a player controlling a criminal around a board representing the streets of London.  It is named after Scotland Yard, the headquarters of London's Metropolitan Police Service.  "Scotland Yard" is an asymmetric board game, with the detective players cooperatively solving a variant of the pursuit-evasion problem.  The game is published by Ravensburger in most of Europe and Canada and by Milton Bradley in the United States.  It received the "Spiel des Jahres" (Game of the Year) award in 1983.

Shengguan Tu | Shengguan Tu (), translated variously as Promoting Officials and Table of Bureaucratic Promotion, is an ancient Chinese board game that originated in the Tang dynasty, with the earliest historical record of a variant of it dating back to 836.  Compared to "Monopoly" and snakes and ladders by contemporary commentators, the game has players take on the roles of mandarins; the objective of the game is to attain the highest possible bureaucratic position, whilst accumulating more prestige and monetary funds than other players.  Historically a gambling game doubling as an educational tool for acquainting Chinese males with the bureaucratic hierarchy, it still enjoys relative popularity nowadays.

7 Wonders (board game) | 7 Wonders is a board game created by Antoine Bauza in 2010 and originally published by Repos Production in Belgium.  "7 Wonders" is a card drafting game that is played using three decks of cards featuring depictions of ancient civilizations, military conflicts and commercial activity.  The game is highly regarded, being one of the highest rated games on the board game discussion website BoardGameGeek.  "7 Wonders" has won a total of more than 30 gaming awards, including the inaugural Kennerspiel des Jahres connoisseurs' award in 2011.
Arno Steinwender | is an Austrian board game designer.  In his free time he develops board games, for which he has won several awards.

Zombies!!! | Zombies!!!  is a tile-based strategy board game for two to six players.  "Zombies!!!"  won the 2001 Origins Award for "Best Graphic Presentation of a Board Game", and Zombies!!!  3: Mall Walkers won 2003's Origins Award for "Best Board Game Expansion".
Dao (game) | Dao is a two-player abstract strategy board game, which won the Mensa Select award in 2001.  It comprises a 4x4 playing board with four playing pieces for each player.

DVONN | DVONN is a two-player strategy board game in which the objective is to accumulate pieces in stacks.  It was released in 2001 by Kris Burm as the fourth game of the "GIPF" Project.  "DVONN" won the 2002 International Gamers Award and the "Games" magazine Game of the Year Award in 2003.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Scotland Yard: passage [1] (Scotland Yard (board game)) states it "received the 'Spiel des Jahres' (Game of the Year) award in 1983." [quote from passage [1]]
  - Shengguan Tu: passage [2] (Shengguan Tu) describes it as an ancient game, e.g., "originated in the Tang dynasty" and notes translations "Promoting Officials" and "Table of Bureaucratic Promotion." No awards are mentioned in passage [2].
- Ambiguities:
  - "Shengguan Tu" appears with variant English titles ("Promoting Officials" / "Table of Bureaucratic Promotion") — passage [2] lists these variants. There is no passage here showing a modern commercial edition or awards for Shengguan Tu; that page may be missing award/modern-publication info.
  - "Scotland Yard" in general could refer to non-game topics, but passage [1] specifically matches the board game page.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entities: "Scotland Yard (board game)" and "Shengguan Tu" (aka "Promoting Officials" / "Table of Bureaucratic Promotion").
  - Precise missing fact needed: the number of awards each game won in calendar year 1983 (i.e., count/list of awards in 1983 for Scotland Yard and for any edition/version of Shengguan Tu).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Scotland Yard (board game)" page — check the lede/infobox and "Awards", "Reception", or "Legacy" sections for a complete list of awards and confirm year(s). Also open "Spiel des Jahres 1983" (or the Spiel des Jahres winners list) to confirm that award and see if other 1983 awards are listed. (Search: "Scotland Yard Spiel des Jahres 1983 awards" / "Scotland Yard awards 1983 site:wikipedia.org")
  2) Open "Shengguan Tu" page (and its alternate-title pages "Promoting Officials" / "Table of Bureaucratic Promotion") — check lede, "Modern versions", "Reception", or "History" for any mention of awards or modern re-publications that might have won awards in 1983. (Search: "Shengguan Tu award 1983" / "Promoting Officials board game award")
  3) If Wikipedia pages lack award lists, fetch each game's BoardGameGeek page and the "List of board game awards by year" or other award databases to get award counts by year (search keywords: "<Game name> BoardGameGeek awards 1983").
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Which awards did Scotland Yard and Shengguan Tu win in 1983?
hop2_docs: Scotland Yard (board game) | Scotland Yard is a board game in which a team of players, as police, cooperate to track down a player controlling a criminal around a board representing the streets of London.  It is named after Scotland Yard, the headquarters of London's Metropolitan Police Service.  "Scotland Yard" is an asymmetric board game, with the detective players cooperatively solving a variant of the pursuit-evasion problem.  The game is published by Ravensburger in most of Europe and Canada and by Milton Bradley in the United States.  It received the "Spiel des Jahres" (Game of the Year) award in 1983.

Shengguan Tu | Shengguan Tu (), translated variously as Promoting Officials and Table of Bureaucratic Promotion, is an ancient Chinese board game that originated in the Tang dynasty, with the earliest historical record of a variant of it dating back to 836.  Compared to "Monopoly" and snakes and ladders by contemporary commentators, the game has players take on the roles of mandarins; the objective of the game is to attain the highest possible bureaucratic position, whilst accumulating more prestige and monetary funds than other players.  Historically a gambling game doubling as an educational tool for acquainting Chinese males with the bureaucratic hierarchy, it still enjoys relative popularity nowadays.

Nine Yards of Other Cloth | "Nine Yards of Other Cloth" is a fantasy story by Manly Wade Wellman.  Originally published in the November 1958 issue of "The Magazine of Fantasy and Science Fiction", it was nominated for, but did not win, the 1959 Hugo Award for Best Short Story.

PFA Scotland Young Player of the Year | The PFA Scotland Young Player of the Year, formerly known as the Scottish PFA Young Player of the Year, is named at the end of every Scottish football season.  The members of the Professional Footballers' Association Scotland vote on which of its young members played the best football in the previous year.  The award was first given in 1978, to Graeme Payne.  The Bulgarian international Stiliyan Petrov was the first non-Scottish player to win the award, when he did so in 2001.

1983 New Orleans Saints season | The 1983 New Orleans Saints season was the team's 17th as a member of the National Football League.  They improved on their previous season's output of 4–5, winning eight games.  Despite the improvement, the team failed to qualify for the playoffs for the seventeenth consecutive season.  With an 8-7 record going into the final game of the season at the Superdome against the Los Angeles Rams, the Saints, with a win, would have finished with their first winning season and their first playoff berth.  However, Rams kicker Mike Lansford kicked a 42-yard field goal with :06 left to defeat the Saints 26-24, and advance to the playoffs.  Other than that field goal, the Rams did not score a single point on offense, instead scoring via a punt return for a touchdown, two interception returns for touchdowns, and a safety.

Alex Grecian | Alex Grecian ( ; born Alexander Douglas Grecian on August 6, 1969) is an American author of short fiction, novels, comic books, and graphic novels.  His notable works include the comic book series Proof and the novels in the Scotland Yard's Murder Squad series: The Yard, The Black Country, The Devil's Workshop, The Harvest Man, Lost and Gone Forever, and The Blue Girl.  He has been nominated for the The Strand Award for Best Debut Novel for "The Yard", the The Dilys Award for "The Black Country", and the Barry Award for Best First Novel for "The Yard".  He was also the recipient of the Kansas Notable Book Award from the State Library of Kansas for "The Yard", "The Black Country", and "The Devil's Workshop".

Tu Vida es Mi Vida | Tu Vida es Mi Vida was spanish language radio program hosted by award-winning motivational speaker Maria Marín and was produced by Cumulus Media Networks en Español (through Cumulus Media).  It has aired on hundreds of spanish radio affiliates throughout the United States.  Tu Vida es Mi Vida came to an end in 2011.
hop2_titles: PFA Scotland Young Player of the Year
response.summary_2: Scotland Yard received the "Spiel des Jahres" (Game of the Year) award in 1983. Shengguan Tu is an ancient Chinese board game that originated in the Tang dynasty, and the provided passages mention no awards for it.
